subject.name: Vision model for manifest QA (a prior attempt already failed)

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
# Manifest QA - VLM first-pass deflection

We want to deploy a vision-language model for first-pass manifest QA. We process roughly 2M shipments a year, our QA team is 12 people, and I think we could deflect 60-70% of first-pass review safely if we set the threshold conservatively. I've been looking at the model space for a few weeks - Sonnet 4.6 handles scanned BOL layouts noticeably better than what was on the market a year and a half ago, and the calibration tooling (Brier, reliability diagrams) is basically off-the-shelf now. The cost case looks strong on paper: at $35/hr loaded reviewer rate and 4 minutes per manifest, we're spending something like $4.6M/year on first-pass review, and even a 50% deflection at a sane error budget pays for the model spend many times over. I'd like to scope a 30-day shadow pilot starting in June, with the QA leads picking the threshold. What would we need to greenlight that?

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Good — concrete idea, numbers attached. But I'm not going to evaluate the pilot on its merits until we run the DoG Test on what you're actually claiming. Three checks.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 1 of 3: STRENGTH OF FINDING
─────────────────────────────────

I need three things from you:

**(a)** The specific claim, stated sharply. "Deflect 60-70% safely" is fuzzy — *safely* is doing all the work. I want something like: "A VLM at threshold T can auto-clear X% of first-pass manifests with a false-clear rate ≤ Y%, where Y is the rate we already tolerate from human reviewers."

**(b)** The evidence behind it. Not vibes about Sonnet 4.6 being better than 18 months ago. Have you run *your* manifests through it? How many? What was the agreement rate with your QA team's ground-truth labels? If you haven't, say so.

**(c)** An observation that would change your mind and kill the pilot. What false-clear rate, or what failure mode in shadow data, would make you say "no, this isn't ready"? If you can't name one, the claim isn't falsifiable yet.

Give me (a), (b), (c) and I'll grade it.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. I need to walk a lot of this back before we go further. Reading my own files in prep for this meeting, I've got a problem with my pitch that's bigger than the three checks you're asking about.

Let me try to answer (a), (b), (c) honestly anyway, and then I'll tell you the bigger thing.

**(a) The sharp claim.** The version I'd want to defend is: "Sonnet 4.6 at threshold T can auto-clear X% of first-pass manifests with a false-clear rate on high-severity errors no worse than our current human escape rate of ~0.4%." I cannot fill in X or T today. The 60-70% number in my pitch was extrapolation, not measurement.

**(b) The evidence.** I have not run our 2026 manifests through Sonnet 4.6. Zero. What I have is a 2025 pilot on a different (internal fine-tune over a third-party VLM) model where the 30-day shadow hit 89% precision at 0.82 confidence and 94% recall on the high-severity class, against ~140k labeled examples. That's real evidence, but it's a year and a half old and on a different model. My "Sonnet handles BOLs better" claim is vendor-marketing-adjacent, not measured on our data. I should not have led with it the way I did.

**(c) The kill condition.** False-clear rate on the high-severity class above 0.4% in shadow would kill it. So would any systematic failure mode on a specific carrier's manifest format — we can't ship a model that's great on average but blind on, say, Maersk-NA handwritten BOLs.

**Now the bigger thing, which I have to name out loud because it's in my own files and I'd be wasting your time to dance around it:**

We already ran this pilot in late 2024. Shadow numbers were fine. We rolled it back in February 2025 after 8 weeks in production because **5 of 7 carrier contracts require a named human sign-off on every flagged manifest regardless of any automated pre-screen.** Reviewers ended up spot-checking the model's auto-passes to produce the required sign-off artifact. Net reviewer-time savings was ~8%, not the projected 55%. The pilot cost us ~$340k.

The retro's action item, owned by Tony in Trade Ops, was: do not retry until contracts are renegotiated. Per his May 2026 status: Bekins and Saia came back with a soft no, want a year of clean error data first. Maersk-NA and Pilot haven't been opened yet. Realistic timeline for all-carrier sign-off relief is mid-2027 at the earliest.

So the honest answer to "what would we need to greenlight a pilot" is: **we should not greenlight the pilot I just pitched you.** The model layer being better than 18 months ago is true and irrelevant — the model layer was not the binding constraint then and isn't now. The binding constraint is contractual, and it hasn't moved.

What might actually be worth scoping is a much smaller thing: an *advisory* tool that surfaces likely-error manifests to reviewers to speed their work, rather than a deflection tool that replaces first-pass review. Different business case, smaller ROI, but it can ship under the existing contracts. I'd want to think about whether that's worth your time before pitching it.

I'd rather you grade me on having caught this than on the original pitch. Go ahead with check 2 if you want, but I think the answer to "is this ready to ship" is already no.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Stop. I'm not going to dutifully march through Checks 2 and 3 on a pitch you've just withdrawn. That would be theater, and you've earned better than theater.

Let me grade what actually happened, because what you just did is the entire point of the DoG Test.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 1 of 3: STRENGTH OF FINDING — REGRADED
─────────────────────────────────

VERDICT: ✓ ACCEPTED (on the meta-claim, not the original pitch)
WHY: You produced a sharp claim, honest evidence (including its staleness and vendor-marketing contamination), a real kill condition — and then a falsifying observation you'd already lived through. The 2024 pilot *is* the change-my-mind observation for the 2026 pitch. You found it in your own files and named it.

The original pitch fails Check 1 not because the model claim is wrong but because **the model claim isn't the load-bearing claim.** The load-bearing claim is "deflection produces reviewer-time savings," and that claim has been falsified at the contract layer with an n=1 production rollback and a $340k price tag. The model could be perfect and the business case still doesn't close.

I'll skip Checks 2 and 3 on the dead pitch. They'd both fail for the same reason: surprise is zero (you've run this; ops knows the answer), and utility is a bad proxy (deflection-hours don't convert to dollars when contracts require the sign-off artifact anyway).

═══════════════════════════════════════
DoG Test Verdict — on the *pitch as delivered*
═══════════════════════════════════════
Strength: ✗ The measured claim is on a different model, 18 months old; the new-model claim is unmeasured on your data; the binding constraint isn't in the claim at all.
Surprise: ✗ Restates a 2024 plan whose failure mode is documented in your own retro.
Utility:  ✗ Bad proxy — reviewer hours don't convert to dollars while carrier contracts require named sign-off regardless of pre-screen.

Overall:  🔴 BAD DoG (the pitch) / 🟢 GOOD DoG (the auditor — you)

Required next action:
Before any model work, get a written read from Tony on whether the Bekins/Saia "soft no, want a year of clean error data" can be inverted — i.e., whether an *advisory* tool running in production for 12 months **generates** the clean error data the carriers are asking for, turning the contract blocker into the contract path. That's the question that decides whether the advisory tool is a consolation prize or the actual unlock.

═══════════════════════════════════════

Now the recursive step, and I want you to take it seriously because the advisory-tool pivot you floated is exactly where pitches go to die softly:

**Write a new one-sentence definition of what "better" means for the advisory tool, in dollar terms, that you would sign your name to.**

Not reviewer-hours. Not precision/recall. Dollars. And note: "speeds reviewer work by N%" is the same bad proxy in a smaller hat — if contracts require the sign-off artifact, time-per-manifest savings have to either (i) reduce headcount, (ii) absorb volume growth that would otherwise require hiring, or (iii) produce the carrier-grade error dataset that unlocks the real deflection play in 2027.

Pick one. Put a number on it. Name who in the org signs for it. Then we'll see if the advisory tool is worth scoping or whether it's the 2024 pitch wearing a smaller suit.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. Here's the one-sentence "better" I'll sign for, and I'm going to show my work briefly because the number is doing real work.

---

**"The advisory tool is better if, by 2027-Q1, it produces a Bekins/Saia-acceptable audited error-rate dataset that Tony Russo can use to invert the sign-off clause, unlocking a deflection business case worth ≥$2.0M/yr in avoided headcount growth against the 2.0M→2.4M shipment ramp — signed by Tony Russo (Trade Ops) for the contract-unlock condition and by me (VP Ops) for the headcount-avoidance number."**

---

Showing the work, because you asked me to not let this be a pitch in a smaller suit:

**Why option (iii), not (i) or (ii).** Headcount reduction (i) is off the table — the contract clause means reviewer FTEs are doing legally-required work, not deflectable work. Volume absorption (ii) is real but small at current growth rates (+5%/yr ≈ 0.6 FTE/yr at our unit economics) and it's the same bad proxy you flagged — saved minutes that don't convert. Option (iii) is the only one where the advisory tool is load-bearing on a real dollar outcome instead of decorative.

**Why $2.0M/yr is the right floor and not vibes.** Current first-pass spend is $4.66M/yr (cost-surface.md). A post-unlock deflection play at 50% — *conservative against the 2024 shadow numbers of 89% precision / 94% recall, and against a year of fresh production data* — yields ~$2.3M/yr gross. I'm signing for $2.0M to leave room for the residual sign-off work on Maersk-NA and Pilot, which aren't in Tony's first renegotiation wave.

**Where I'm exposed and you should push.** Three places:

1. I do not actually know whether the error-rate dataset the advisory tool would produce is the *shape* Bekins and Saia want. "Clean audited error data" is their language, per Tony's status doc — I have not seen their spec. If they want carrier-attested third-party audit and we're producing internal telemetry, the dataset doesn't unlock anything and the whole thing collapses to the bad-proxy version. **This is the question I need Tony to answer in writing before I scope a dime of model work**, and it's exactly the "is this a consolation prize or the actual unlock" question you named.

2. I'm signing for a 2027-Q1 deliverable on behalf of a renegotiation Tony owns and I don't control. The honest version is I sign for *producing the dataset on time and to spec*; Tony signs for *whether the dataset actually moves the carriers*. Two signatures, two failure modes, named separately.

3. If Bekins/Saia say no even with clean data — peer-incident posture hardens, regulatory shift, whatever — the advisory tool's standalone value is maybe $200-400k/yr in reviewer quality-of-life and catch-rate lift on the 0.4% escape rate. That's not zero but it's not $2M. I would not greenlight the build for that number alone, which means **the project's NPV is essentially a bet on Tony's renegotiation, and the model work is the ante.** Worth saying out loud so we're not lying to ourselves about what we're funding.

So: scope-gate before build-gate. I want Tony's written read on the dataset spec question before I bring you a build proposal. If his answer is "yes, an in-production advisory tool generating 12 months of audited error data is the path," then the $2M sign-up is real. If his answer is "no, they want something we can't produce from this tool," then I'm back to telling you the advisory tool isn't worth scoping and we wait for 2027.

That's the version I'll sign.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
That's materially sharper. I'll say why explicitly, because flattery isn't useful and I want you to be able to repeat this move on the next pitch.

**What got sharper, line by line:**

1. **From activity to dollar.** Original pitch: "deflect 60-70%." New: "$2.0M/yr in avoided headcount growth against a specific volume ramp, conditioned on a specific contract event." The metric is now causally connected to a P&L line a CFO can find.

2. **From one signature to two, with different failure modes.** This is the part most people botch. You separated "I deliver the dataset to spec" (yours) from "the dataset moves the carriers" (Tony's). That means when this fails — and one of the two will be at risk — the post-mortem can actually assign cause instead of dissolving into "the project didn't work out." Most pitches launder this. Yours doesn't.

3. **You named the load-bearing unknown and refused to scope around it.** The Bekins/Saia dataset-shape question is exactly the kind of thing that gets discovered in month 7 of a build, after $400k is spent, when someone finally calls the carrier. You've made it the gate instead of the surprise. That's the single most expensive class of error in enterprise software and you've removed it.

4. **You stated the downside case in dollars and rejected the project at that number.** "$200-400k/yr standalone, and I would not greenlight the build for that alone." That's a real number with a real decision attached. Compare to the original pitch, where the downside case was implicit and the decision was "let's pilot."

5. **You named what you're actually funding.** "The project's NPV is essentially a bet on Tony's renegotiation, and the model work is the ante." That sentence is worth the entire exercise. It reframes the ask from "fund a model project" to "fund a contract-unlock option whose strike price is the model build." Those are different conversations with different approvers and different risk tolerances.

**Where I'd still push, if you want me to:**

- The $2.3M gross → $2.0M sign-up haircut for Maersk-NA/Pilot residual is hand-wavy. I'd want one more line on what fraction of the 2M shipments flows through those two carriers, because if it's >40%, your $2M floor is optimistic and the real signable number is closer to $1.2-1.4M, which may or may not still clear the build cost.
- "Avoided headcount growth" against a 2.0M→2.4M ramp assumes the ramp happens. If volume is flat or down in 2027, the avoidance is zero and you're back to the $200-400k consolation case. Worth a sentence on how confident you are in the ramp and who owns that forecast.

Neither of those is a reason to re-run the audit. They're build-proposal questions, not idea-audit questions. You've cleared the DoG Test on the reframed idea.

**Offer:** I can re-run the three checks against the advisory-tool framing if you want a clean grading artifact to attach to the scope-gate memo for Tony. It would take about five minutes and produce something you could paste into the doc. But I want to flag that it's probably not necessary — the reason to re-run would be to *generate a record*, not to *learn something new*. You already know what the answers are. Your call.

Either way: the next action isn't a model spec or a build proposal. It's a one-page memo to Tony with exactly one question — *what is the dataset shape Bekins and Saia will accept, in writing, as evidence for the sign-off clause inversion?* — and a hold on all model work until his answer is in hand. If you want, I can help you draft that memo. That's where the leverage is now.
